APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work falls squarely within the NLP/ML venue scope: it introduces a new annotated corpus for argumentative revision, a clearly relevant resource for argument mining and writing analysis, and demonstrates automatic classification experiments. However, from an experimental-reproducibility standpoint the paper has gaps (no reported hyperparameters or code, only a unigram baseline and limited ablations), which weakens its immediate utility to the community even though the dataset and annotation schema are valuable. Overall it is a good fit but would benefit from stronger reproducibility and more competitive baselines to be a perfect fit. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it presents a novel, well-motivated corpus and a fine-grained revision annotation schema directly useful to argument mining and writing-support research. The problem formulation (sentence-level alignment and purposeful revision labeling across drafts) addresses questions of interest to the conference audience, and the experiments (classification with cross-validation and analysis of revision behavior) use standard evaluation practices. While some practical details (code release, hyperparameters) and ecological coverage (limited to college students) are noted as limitations, the resource and analyses are positioned to enable follow-up modeling and educational applications, making the work highly relevant to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work falls squarely within the NLP/ML venue scope: it introduces a new annotated corpus for argumentative revision, a clearly relevant resource for argument mining and writing analysis, and demonstrates automatic classification experiments. However, from an experimental-reproducibility standpoint the paper has gaps (no reported hyperparameters or code, only a unigram baseline and limited ablations), which weakens its immediate utility to the community even though the dataset and annotation schema are valuable. Overall it is a good fit but would benefit from stronger reproducibility and more competitive baselines to be a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it presents a novel, well-motivated corpus and a fine-grained revision annotation schema directly useful to argument mining and writing-support research. The problem formulation (sentence-level alignment and purposeful revision labeling across drafts) addresses questions of interest to the conference audience, and the experiments (classification with cross-validation and analysis of revision behavior) use standard evaluation practices. While some practical details (code release, hyperparameters) and ecological coverage (limited to college students) are noted as limitations, the resource and analyses are positioned to enable follow-up modeling and educational applications, making the work highly relevant to the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates automatic revision-purpose classification using the new corpus but compares only against a unigram baseline and an SVM with a limited feature set, which omits stronger contemporary baselines (e.g., richer feature ablations, neural models) and hyperparameter reporting. The evaluation appears fair in that experiments use the same data and metric (10-fold CV and unweighted F), and related work is discussed, but the comparisons are incomplete and lack deeper analysis of why the chosen models outperform (or fail) relative to alternatives. To be fully convincing the study should include additional competitive baselines, ablation studies, and clearer acknowledgement when prior methods might match or exceed reported performance. | B(3): The paper evaluates automatic classification on its new dataset using a unigram baseline and SVM with feature groups on the same data and metric (unweighted F), so experimental conditions appear fair. However, comparing only to a simple unigram baseline is limited given recent supervised and neural approaches for revision/purpose classification; the paper does not report comparisons to stronger or state-of-the-art baselines nor hyperparameter details. Related work is discussed adequately, but the empirical comparisons are incomplete, reducing confidence in claims about model effectiveness relative to prior methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates automatic revision-purpose classification using the new corpus but compares only against a unigram baseline and an SVM with a limited feature set, which omits stronger contemporary baselines (e.g., richer feature ablations, neural models) and hyperparameter reporting. The evaluation appears fair in that experiments use the same data and metric (10-fold CV and unweighted F), and related work is discussed, but the comparisons are incomplete and lack deeper analysis of why the chosen models outperform (or fail) relative to alternatives. To be fully convincing the study should include additional competitive baselines, ablation studies, and clearer acknowledgement when prior methods might match or exceed reported performance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates automatic classification on its new dataset using a unigram baseline and SVM with feature groups on the same data and metric (unweighted F), so experimental conditions appear fair. However, comparing only to a simple unigram baseline is limited given recent supervised and neural approaches for revision/purpose classification; the paper does not report comparisons to stronger or state-of-the-art baselines nor hyperparameter details. Related work is discussed adequately, but the empirical comparisons are incomplete, reducing confidence in claims about model effectiveness relative to prior methods.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important, under-resourced problem—fine-grained annotated revisions across three drafts with sentence alignments and a purpose schema—which is directly useful for argument mining and writing-revision research. The corpus and schema are likely to change how researchers study revision behavior and enable supervised models (they demonstrate baseline SVM results), and they open clear new directions for modeling multi-draft revision trajectories. Impact is somewhat constrained by the dataset’s focus on college essays and the paper’s limited reproducibility details (no reported hyperparameters or code), so I judge it strong but not transformative. | B(3): This corpus addresses a meaningful need in argument mining and educational NLP by providing sentence-aligned, multi-draft argumentative essays with a clear revision-purpose schema (coarse Surface vs. Content plus eight fine-grained categories) and demonstrated use cases (behavioral analysis and automatic classification). It is likely to be a useful resource for training and evaluation and to open follow-on work on revision modeling and pedagogical interventions, but its impact is moderated by scope limitations (college-student-only data, no direct measures of revision quality/improvement) and relatively modest baseline experiments, which make it more of a solid, reusable contribution than a field-changing one.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important, under-resourced problem—fine-grained annotated revisions across three drafts with sentence alignments and a purpose schema—which is directly useful for argument mining and writing-revision research. The corpus and schema are likely to change how researchers study revision behavior and enable supervised models (they demonstrate baseline SVM results), and they open clear new directions for modeling multi-draft revision trajectories. Impact is somewhat constrained by the dataset’s focus on college essays and the paper’s limited reproducibility details (no reported hyperparameters or code), so I judge it strong but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This corpus addresses a meaningful need in argument mining and educational NLP by providing sentence-aligned, multi-draft argumentative essays with a clear revision-purpose schema (coarse Surface vs. Content plus eight fine-grained categories) and demonstrated use cases (behavioral analysis and automatic classification). It is likely to be a useful resource for training and evaluation and to open follow-on work on revision modeling and pedagogical interventions, but its impact is moderated by scope limitations (college-student-only data, no direct measures of revision quality/improvement) and relatively modest baseline experiments, which make it more of a solid, reusable contribution than a field-changing one.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well-organized and the main contributions (new three-draft corpus, annotation schema, and two applications) are stated clearly early on. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and the methods/experiments follow a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Presentation suffers only minor gaps for a reproducibility-minded reader: hyperparameters for the SVM and code availability are not reported, and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables fully clarify the alignment and annotation examples. Addressing those omissions would make the presentation exceptional. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the new three-draft annotated corpus, the revision schema, and application experiments—are stated early and carried through. Notation and labels appear consistent and the writing quality is good; figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. A few minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters and code availability are not reported), but overall clarity and flow are strong.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized and the main contributions (new three-draft corpus, annotation schema, and two applications) are stated clearly early on. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, and the methods/experiments follow a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—the new three-draft annotated corpus, the revision schema, and application experiments—are stated early and carried through. Notation and labels appear consistent and the writing quality is good; figures/tables and examples are described as effective and informative. A few minor presentation gaps remain (e.g., hyperparameters and code availability are not reported), but overall clarity and flow are strong.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper clearly states and largely delivers on its primary claim of creating a three-draft, sentence-aligned revision corpus with coarse and fine-grained labels and demonstrates feasibility of automatic classification (SVM, unigram baseline, Location/Textual/Language features, evaluated with 10-fold CV and unweighted average F-score). However, experimental methodology has weaknesses that limit confidence: only a unigram baseline is compared, no hyperparameters or code are reported, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. These omissions and limited baselines are significant reproducibility and validity concerns, so while the dataset and analyses are valuable the technical evaluation is only partially convincing. | B(4): The paper’s main claims (introducing a new three-draft argumentative revision corpus, a two-level annotation schema, and demonstrating analyses plus automatic classification) are clearly stated and supported by the described annotation effort and experiments. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental setup is reasonable (SVM with unigram baseline, feature groups, 10-fold CV, UA F-score) but is weakened by limited baselines, lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of statistical significance testing. Overall the conclusions are largely justified by the presented results, though stronger experimental controls and more comprehensive baselines would improve confidence in the automatic-classification claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states and largely delivers on its primary claim of creating a three-draft, sentence-aligned revision corpus with coarse and fine-grained labels and demonstrates feasibility of automatic classification (SVM, unigram baseline, Location/Textual/Language features, evaluated with 10-fold CV and unweighted average F-score). However, experimental methodology has weaknesses that limit confidence: only a unigram baseline is compared, no hyperparameters or code are reported, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. These omissions and limited baselines are significant reproducibility and validity concerns, so while the dataset and analyses are valuable the technical evaluation is only partially convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (introducing a new three-draft argumentative revision corpus, a two-level annotation schema, and demonstrating analyses plus automatic classification) are clearly stated and supported by the described annotation effort and experiments. There are no mathematical derivations to check. The experimental setup is reasonable (SVM with unigram baseline, feature groups, 10-fold CV, UA F-score) but is weakened by limited baselines, lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of statistical significance testing. Overall the conclusions are largely justified by the presented results, though stronger experimental controls and more comprehensive baselines would improve confidence in the automatic-classification claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper delivers a valuable new three-draft corpus with sentence alignments and fine-grained revision labels and demonstrates utility via descriptive analysis and revision-purpose classification (SVM, unigram baseline, additional feature groups, 10-fold CV). However, experimental support is incomplete for strong claims: there are no reported hyperparameters or code, no inter-annotator agreement or annotation protocol details in the summary, and only a single simple baseline with no ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature groups or modeling choices. Related work is described as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility and deeper error analysis are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but missing important elements for full confidence and reuse. | B(3): The paper provides a useful new corpus with sentence-level alignments and fine-grained revision labels and demonstrates both analysis and automatic classification using an SVM baseline with 10-fold CV, which adequately supports the claim that the resource can enable such studies. However, experimental depth is limited: only a unigram baseline and a single classifier are reported, no ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature groups or hyperparameters are given, and reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters) are not mentioned. The authors do acknowledge some dataset limitations (student population, lack of direct improvement judgments), but more comprehensive experiments and release details would be needed for stronger empirical support.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper delivers a valuable new three-draft corpus with sentence alignments and fine-grained revision labels and demonstrates utility via descriptive analysis and revision-purpose classification (SVM, unigram baseline, additional feature groups, 10-fold CV). However, experimental support is incomplete for strong claims: there are no reported hyperparameters or code, no inter-annotator agreement or annotation protocol details in the summary, and only a single simple baseline with no ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature groups or modeling choices. Related work is described as adequate and some limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility and deeper error analysis are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but missing important elements for full confidence and reuse.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a useful new corpus with sentence-level alignments and fine-grained revision labels and demonstrates both analysis and automatic classification using an SVM baseline with 10-fold CV, which adequately supports the claim that the resource can enable such studies. However, experimental depth is limited: only a unigram baseline and a single classifier are reported, no ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature groups or hyperparameters are given, and reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters) are not mentioned. The authors do acknowledge some dataset limitations (student population, lack of direct improvement judgments), but more comprehensive experiments and release details would be needed for stronger empirical support.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a notable and concrete contribution in the form of a new three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a targeted annotation schema (coarse Surface vs. Content plus eight fine-grained categories), which is not merely an application of off‑the‑shelf methods. While the core tasks (revision classification, analysis of student behavior) are well-studied, the dataset and annotation perspective fills a practical gap for argument mining and revision research and is clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary. The experimental methods themselves are straightforward (SVM baseline, unigram baseline), so novelty rests on the corpus and schema rather than algorithmic innovation; that makes the contribution notable but not a radical methodological advance. | B(4): The primary novelty lies in the resource: a three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a purpose schema that distinguishes Surface vs. Content changes plus eight fine-grained categories—this offers a new empirical perspective for argument mining and writing-revision research. The methodological side (SVM classification with unigram and feature groups) is routine, so methodological innovation is limited, but the dataset and label scheme are clearly differentiated from prior single-draft or non-argument-focused revision corpora. For an expert in argumentation mining and educational NLP, the corpus would be a notable and useful addition that enables new analyses and modeling tasks, even if the modeling experiments themselves are incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a notable and concrete contribution in the form of a new three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a targeted annotation schema (coarse Surface vs. Content plus eight fine-grained categories), which is not merely an application of off‑the‑shelf methods. While the core tasks (revision classification, analysis of student behavior) are well-studied, the dataset and annotation perspective fills a practical gap for argument mining and revision research and is clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary. The experimental methods themselves are straightforward (SVM baseline, unigram baseline), so novelty rests on the corpus and schema rather than algorithmic innovation; that makes the contribution notable but not a radical methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: The primary novelty lies in the resource: a three-draft argumentative revision corpus with sentence-level alignments and a purpose schema that distinguishes Surface vs. Content changes plus eight fine-grained categories—this offers a new empirical perspective for argument mining and writing-revision research. The methodological side (SVM classification with unigram and feature groups) is routine, so methodological innovation is limited, but the dataset and label scheme are clearly differentiated from prior single-draft or non-argument-focused revision corpora. For an expert in argumentation mining and educational NLP, the corpus would be a notable and useful addition that enables new analyses and modeling tasks, even if the modeling experiments themselves are incremental.

main_claims: Introduces a new corpus of annotated revisions for argumentative essays, containing three drafts per essay with sentence-level alignments and labeled revision purposes.
Provides a revision annotation schema separating Surface vs. Content-based changes and eight finer-grained categories relevant to argument mining and discourse analysis.
Demonstrates two applications of the corpus: analysis of student revision behavior and training/evaluation of automatic revision-purpose classification models.
_summary.methodology: Collected three drafts of college students' argumentative essays; sentences were automatically aligned then manually corrected and annotated by trained annotators into coarse (Surface vs. Content) and eight fine-grained revision categories. For automatic classification experiments they used an SVM with a unigram baseline and additional feature groups (Location, Textual, Language) evaluated via 10-fold cross-validation reporting unweighted average F-score.
datasets: Argumentative Revision Corpus (three-draft essays from college students)
limitations: No evaluative data on improvement of drafts (they approximate improvement by number of revisions).
Current corpus focuses on college students (native and L2) and may need augmentation for finer-grained categories and other properties.

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Experimentalist
reviewer_a.background: ML/NLP engineer and researcher with extensive experience designing and reproducing empirical studies in text-mining and supervised classification tasks; has led replication efforts and contributed to shared-task datasets and baseline toolkits.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental details (data splits, hyperparameters, preprocessing, code availability)
values strong and relevant baselines, ablation studies, and error analysis to support claims about model performance
looks for appropriate evaluation metrics and statistical significance when comparing systems
notices annotation protocol details, inter-annotator agreement, and potential data leakage or annotation artifacts
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and reproducibility, asking for additional analyses or clarifications that would make results trustworthy and actionable for future work.

reviewer_b.name: Argumentation & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in argumentation mining and educational NLP, with a track record of corpus creation, annotation schema design, and studies of writing pedagogy and feedback systems.
traits: values novelty and usefulness of annotation schemas relative to existing corpora and theoretical frameworks
notices coverage and ecological validity: whether the dataset and labels reflect real argumentative behaviors and support downstream scientific or educational use
looks for clear articulation of intended use cases and how the resource advances the field (e.g., new research questions enabled)
attentive to annotation quality, label granularity, and alignment with discourse and argumentation literature
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper primarily for its contribution to the research landscape and practical impact, assessing whether the corpus, schema, and analyses meaningfully advance argument mining and writing-support applications.
